bugs.error_message: TypeError: No Python class registered for C++ class Widget
bugs.root_cause: Boost.Python type converters for Widget (registered by Spike.so) were not yet loaded when the embedder attempted object(boost::python::ptr(&cpp_w)). The module must be imported before pushing C++ objects of that type into Python.
bugs.fix: Added explicit `import("Spike")` call in main.cpp immediately after setting sys.path and before the C++->Python push. The separate Spike.so module runs its BOOST_PYTHON_MODULE initializer on import, registering Widget's converters in the shared registry.

bugs.error_message: Python 2→3 port: #include <python2.6/Python.h> not found; cStringIO removed in Python 3; boost::filesystem path methods return path not string
bugs.root_cause: Scripting module was written for Python 2.6 / Boost 1.40. Three breakages on Python 3.11 / Boost 1.81: (1) <python2.6/Python.h> path gone, (2) cStringIO module removed in Python 3, (3) boost::filesystem path::extension()/stem() now return path objects instead of std::string.
bugs.fix: (1) Changed #include <python2.6/Python.h> to #include <Python.h>. (2) Changed cStringIO.StringIO() to io.StringIO() in embedded Python exec string. (3) Added .string() after ruta_path.extension() in InterpretePython.cpp and after ruta_path.stem() in ModuloPython.cpp.

bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: PyString_Check should be PyBytes_Check
Wrong reference: cpp should be hpp
Wrong reference: cpp should be hpp

bugs.root_cause: PyString_Check was removed in Python 3; code used Python 2 API

bugs.error_message: no member named ob_type in _typeobject (container_conversions.h Python 3)
bugs.root_cause: Python 3 makes PyTypeObject opaque; ob_type->ob_type is inaccessible
bugs.fix: Replaced obj_ptr->ob_type->ob_type access with Py_TYPE(Py_TYPE(obj_ptr)) which is valid in Python 2 and 3

bugs.root_cause: Original code included .cpp files directly (Python 2 era workaround). With CMake build Reglas is a shared lib; including the .cpp was wrong
bugs.fix: Changed to include the corresponding .hpp headers instead

bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: maxint should be maxsize
Wrong reference: maxint should be maxsize
Wrong reference: vector should be typeinfo

bugs.root_cause: No early return/throw for edge case: path_void == nullptr

bugs.error_message: SIGSEGV deep inside the C++ minimax recursion when TipoDeJugada.MOVIMIENTO is requested; surfaces e.g. in `consola bin/agente_astar.py bin/agenteMiniMax2.py` after ~448 lines of board renders, well into the match
bugs.root_cause: KNOWN-BAD CODE since 2010 — documented at hg commit message 1314ada (`Casi se termina de implementar el minimax en c++. Hay fuga de memoria y no funca muy bien si se está haciendo MOVIMIENTO`). The C++ minimax in Reglas/Minimax.{cpp,hpp} has a self-documented memory leak / correctness problem in the MOVIMIENTO branch. Was incidentally masked under D1 by an unrelated A* code path (the magic-82 NO_SOLUTION-as-SOLVED hack in Astar.cpp) that prevented MiniMax2's astar IndexError fallback from firing, so the buggy minimax(MOVIMIENTO) path was rarely exercised. The D1' fixes PR fixed the A* hack to be honest about NO_SOLUTION → empty path → IndexError fires more often → MiniMax2 now reaches the broken minimax(MOVIMIENTO) recursion → SIGSEGV.
bugs.fix: UNRESOLVED — DEFER. Out of scope for the D1' fixes PR. Next steps for a follow-up minimax debug session: (1) run consola under lldb with astar-vs-MiniMax2 to capture a stack trace at the SIGSEGV site; (2) review Reglas::Minimax::minimax for the MOVIMIENTO branch's lifetime and memory ownership; (3) consider whether the agent's IndexError fallback should be a no-op or a safer move-of-last-resort rather than calling C++ minimax. agente_astar.py uses random.choice as its fallback — that pattern survives; MiniMax2 should adopt something similar until the C++ minimax is fixed.

bugs.error_message: Reglas::SinHijo raised from bin/agenteCamina.py(36) during siguienteJugada; surfaces e.g. in `consola bin/agenteCamina.py bin/agenteMiniMax2.py` once MiniMax2's barrier placement shapes the board such that Camina's next-cell-in-self.direccion has no child. Caught cleanly by the D1' top-level catch in consola/main.cpp → exit 1 with diagnostic, not SIGABRT.
bugs.root_cause: Camina is a naive walker: each turn it calls `celdaActual.getHijo(self.direccion).estaLibre()` (line 36) with NO guard for cells where `getHijo(direccion)` throws SinHijo (i.e. when celdaActual is on the edge of the board in self.direccion, OR when a barrier blocks that direction with no underlying child set). The agent assumes there is ALWAYS a child cell in self.direccion, which holds against another Camina (both walk straight, never block) but fails as soon as a barrier-placing opponent (MiniMax2 / MiniMax4) blocks Camina's forward path with no usable detour set.
bugs.fix: UNRESOLVED — DEFER. Out of scope for the D1' fixes PR (this is an agent-logic bug, not infrastructure). Two reasonable follow-up options: (1) make Camina catch Reglas.SinHijo and fall back to a side-step or random.choice from ayudante.getMovimientosPosibles(self.id); (2) probe celdaActual.estaLibreDireccion(self.direccion) first and only call getHijo when true. Note: ONBOARDING.md §4 pins Camina-vs-MiniMax2 as the canonical run recipe — but D1's actual acceptance test was Camina-vs-Camina (the pair that does succeed). Worth updating ONBOARDING.md to point at a pair that actually completes (e.g. astar-vs-astar) until Camina is fixed.

bugs.root_cause: No early return/throw for edge case: !rf

bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: smgr should be dev
no member named 'setMaterialFlag' in 'irr::scene::IAnimatedMeshSceneNode'; no member named 'EMF_LIGHTING' in namespace 'irr::video'
bugs.root_cause: IrrlichtMt 1.9.0mt15 (Minetest fork) removed the classic fixed-function material-flag API: ISceneNode::setMaterialFlag() and the entire E_MATERIAL_FLAG enum (EMF_LIGHTING etc., EMaterialFlags.h is gone). The D2.0 plan's main.cpp was written against classic Irrlicht 1.8 (boardNode->setMaterialFlag(video::EMF_LIGHTING, false)).
bugs.fix: Set the SMaterial::Lighting bool member directly on every material of the node. The fork provides ISceneNode::forEachMaterial(F&&) (template helper in ISceneNode.h, docstring: 'if you want the whole mesh to be affected by light') and SMaterial has a public bool Lighting field (default true). Replaced the line with: boardNode->forEachMaterial([](video::SMaterial& mat) { mat.Lighting = false; }); — compiles and runs. NEW verified IrrlichtMt API difference beyond Task 1's getMesh/getFileSystem/createScreenShot list: classic material flags are gone; mutate SMaterial fields (Lighting, Wireframe, BackfaceCulling, etc.) directly via getMaterial(i)/getMaterialCount() or forEachMaterial.

bugs.error_message: board.png rendered with only one corner of the Tablero peeking into the bottom-left; most of the frame empty
bugs.root_cause: The plan's hardcoded camera (pos (220,550,-120), target (220,0,220)) assumed the board mesh is centred near grid origin (~220,220). But Tablero.obj's model origin sits at a far CORNER: getBoundingBox() reports center=(-278,31,-275), extent=(657,63,650) — the board occupies the NEGATIVE-XZ quadrant. The camera aimed at empty space in +XZ, so only the corner nearest origin was in frame.
bugs.fix: Compute the camera from the mesh's actual bounding box instead of hardcoded grid coords: c = board->getBoundingBox().getCenter(); reach = max(extentX, extentZ); cam pos = (c.X, c.Y + reach*1.4, c.Z - reach*0.8), target = c. Re-run confirmed the whole board renders centred (perspective trapezoid). FINDING for Task 3: cellToWorld() in scene_builder.cpp maps cell (0,0)->world (0,0,0), but the board mesh centre is at world (-278,_,-275) — the grid-to-world mapping must account for the board's corner origin (offset cells, or recentre the board node) or pieces will sit off the board.

bugs.root_cause: Was returning: core::vector3df(x * spacing, 0.0f, y * spacing);

bugs.fix: Rewrote 18→27 lines (6 removed) | Also: addMesh(dev, "conf/skin_default/Tablero.obj", core; addMesh(dev, "conf/skin_default/Celda.obj", cellTo

bugs.root_cause: IrrlichtMt 1.9.0mt15 (Minetest fork) removed the entire dynamic-light scene-node API: ISceneManager::addLightSceneNode() and ILightSceneNode are gone (Minetest does its own lighting). Only ISceneManager::setAmbientLight(const video::SColorf&) survives. Task 3 first tried to add a light node so the lit meshes would show shading.
bugs.fix: Dropped addLightSceneNode entirely. Meshes cannot be dynamically lit; depth reads from camera angle + occlusion. To still colour them, kept Lighting=true and set per-material EmissiveColor with a near-black setAmbientLight, so output = EmissiveColor (flat per-mesh colour). NEW verified IrrlichtMt API difference beyond Task 1/2 (getMesh/getFileSystem/createScreenShot, setMaterialFlag/EMF_*): no dynamic lights; only setAmbientLight.

bugs.error_message: All meshes render the same flat light-gray; board/cells/pieces/barrier indistinguishable in the screenshot, and only the front rank of cells is visible
bugs.root_cause: Three compounding issues. (1) Every converted .mtl is white (Kd ~1,1,1; Celda/Barrera/Tablero exactly 1 1 1, Mono 0.90), so with no per-node colour every mesh is the same gray. (2) With Lighting=false the IrrlichtMt fixed-function Solid path modulates output by VERTEX COLOUR/texture and IGNORES SMaterial::DiffuseColor/EmissiveColor — setting those while unlit had no visible effect; meshes stayed white. (3) Cells (Y in [0,10]) were placed at world Y=0, but the recentred board straddles Y=0 (top surface ~+31.5), so 80 of 81 cells were buried INSIDE the board and only the front rank poked out.
bugs.fix: (1)+(2) Set Lighting=TRUE and assign a distinct per-mesh-type EmissiveColor (+Ambient/Diffuse) in addMesh, with main.cpp calling setAmbientLight(black). Lit output then = EmissiveColor: a flat distinct colour per mesh ignoring the white .mtl. Colours: board dark-brown, cells tan, p0 red, p1 blue, barrier green. (3) Introduced kBoardTopY=31.5 (board half-height after recentring) as the grid-plane Y in cellToWorld so cells rest ON the board surface and pieces lift above it. Result: clean 9x9 tan grid on a brown board, red+blue pawns at opposite ends, green barrier centre.
